title: Frozen Backbones as Feature Extractors
concept: A frozen pretrained CNN backbone can act as a fixed feature extractor while a new task-specific head learns on top, making transfer learning faster, cheaper, and safer on small datasets.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what is the main thing a pretrained CNN brings to a new vision task?

options.text: Useful learned visual features from earlier large-scale training
A guarantee that no task-specific training is needed
A new camera sensor inside the model

exercises.explain: Right — now the question is how much of that pretrained backbone you should leave unchanged.

cards.title: Keep the eyes, swap the job description
cards.body: Sometimes the pretrained CNN already sees the world well enough. You do not need to rewrite its visual instincts. You can keep the backbone fixed and only train a new head that turns those features into your new labels.

cards.title: What a frozen backbone setup means
cards.body: In feature-extractor mode, the pretrained CNN backbone is frozen, meaning its weights do not update during the new training run. A new classifier or regression head is placed on top, and only that top section learns the target task.

cards.title: Keep the microscope, change the checklist
cards.body: Imagine a lab already owns an excellent microscope. For a new experiment, you do not rebuild the optics. You keep the microscope and change what the scientist is looking for. A frozen backbone is the microscope; the new head is the checklist.

cards.title: Leaf disease classifier with a frozen CNN
cards.body: A team has only a few hundred labeled plant images. They take a pretrained EfficientNet or ResNet, freeze the convolutional backbone, and train a small head for six disease classes. The backbone still extracts textures, edges, and shape clues; the new head learns how those clues map to the plant labels.

cards.title: Frozen backbone does not mean zero learning
cards.body: A common myth is that once the backbone is frozen, the whole system stops learning. Not true. The new head still updates, so the model can learn the task-specific decision rule even while the borrowed visual features stay fixed.

cards.title: Who learns in this setup?
cards.body: Different parts of the model play different roles during feature-extractor transfer learning.

left.text:
Pretrained CNN layers stay fixed
Provide visual features from the image
No weight updates during new training

right.text:
New top layers update on the task
Map features to new labels or scores
Usually small and cheap to train

exercises.prompt: When is a frozen-backbone baseline often a very sensible first choice?

options.text: When the dataset is small and you want a quick, stable starting point
When you want every pretrained weight to change immediately
When you believe the task head should be deleted entirely
When you need to prove pretraining is unnecessary

exercises.explain: Correct — freezing the backbone is often the safest low-compute baseline when labels are limited.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In feature-extractor mode, the new head can still learn even though the pretrained backbone is frozen.
exercises.explain: True — only the backbone is fixed; the head is still trainable.

exercises.sentence: In a frozen-backbone setup, you usually train a new task-specific ___.
exercises.explain: Right — the backbone extracts features, and the head turns them into predictions.

exercises.prompt: Which action best matches using a pretrained CNN as a feature extractor?

options.text: Freeze the backbone, attach a new head, and train the head on the new labels
Delete the backbone because reused features would only get in the way
Freeze the backbone and also refuse to train any head at all

exercises.explain: Yes — that is the classic feature-extractor recipe.

exercises.prompt: You have 400 labeled bird images and limited compute. What is the best first transfer-learning baseline?

options.text: Use a pretrained CNN backbone as a frozen feature extractor and train only a small new head
Ignore pretraining and build a deep CNN from random weights immediately
Freeze every layer and also skip training the head
Replace the images with text descriptions so the backbone is unnecessary

exercises.explain: Exactly. A frozen backbone gives you strong borrowed features without spending much compute or demanding lots of labels. It is often the practical first baseline before trying deeper adaptation. Next up: what changes when you decide the borrowed backbone itself should adapt.
